title: Public Value and the Risk of New Global Inequality

lines.text: Section six. Public value, and the risk of new global inequality.
Synthetic biology promises to deliver real public value — cheaper medicines, more affordable materials, sustainable fuels, environmentally responsible chemical production.
All of these are genuine goods, and the field's advocates have been articulate about them.
But the actual distribution of those benefits — who gets them, on what terms, at what cost to whom — is not automatic.

Industrial sciences and technologies have a long history of producing wealth in ways that exacerbate global inequalities rather than reduce them, and the chapter argues that synthetic biology must be examined honestly for the same risks.
Three areas of concern are flagged.
Concern one: food security.
The first and second generations of biofuels are produced from biomass — switchgrass, corn, sugarcane.
If financial incentives push agricultural producers to grow energy crops rather than food crops, food shortages in poor countries can be aggravated.
Some commentators argue that a third generation of biofuels, using metabolic engineering of microbes or algae grown in vats, would sidestep this problem.
But algal-based methods may still require sugar-rich feedstocks, which would reintroduce the same tension between energy and food.
The optimistic view of third-generation biofuels has not yet been fully realised in practice.
Concern two: ecological sustainability.
The release of engineered organisms into agricultural landscapes — engineered crops, engineered soil microbes — could have ecological consequences we cannot predict in advance.
Beyond the direct biosafety concerns we discussed earlier, there are longer-term sustainability concerns.
If engineered organisms enable intensive agriculture on previously marginal lands, the result may be short-term productivity gains followed by long-term ecological degradation.
The historical pattern with genetically modified crops gives some warning here, even though synthetic biology's specific implementations may differ.
Concern three: economic dislocation.
When synthetic biology displaces a natural product, the producers of the natural product may be left worse off — even if the consumers benefit.
The textbook's example is artemisinin.
Artemisinin's medical effect was first published by Chinese scientists in the 1980s.
The plant Artemisia annua, which produces artemisinin naturally, is endemic to southern China, but also grows in India, Vietnam, Pakistan, and South Africa.

The intellectual property rights for artemisinin manufacturing methods are largely held by Western pharmaceutical companies — Novartis, Sanofi — so Chinese artemisinin producers, ironically, must pay patent fees to Western companies when exporting their product.

Now that synthetic biology has enabled semi-synthetic artemisinin production via engineered yeast, Chinese artemisinin farmers are exposed to direct competition from a process they did not develop, controlled by Western companies, that may displace their traditional industry.
China's artemisinin exports dropped 24 percent in 2009.

The story shows how a successful synthetic biology application — semi-synthetic artemisinin saving lives by making the drug more affordable for malarial patients — can simultaneously dislocate the existing producer economy in ways that fall hardest on the people least able to absorb the dislocation.
Whether the net effect is positive or negative depends on whether the displaced producers are supported through the transition, and on whether the cost savings reach the patients who need them most.
Neither is guaranteed.
Reflecting on these concerns, the authors argue that the responsible development of synthetic biology requires attention to issues beyond the immediate technical goals.
How should land use be governed when synthetic-biology-enabled crops change agricultural economics?
How should biomass be acquired responsibly?
How should the social and economic dislocations of new technologies be absorbed by the people most affected?
Synthetic biology might shift the distribution of benefits across global geography — and whether that shift creates new inequalities depends on choices being made now.
